{"step_by_step_thinking": "The query asks whether residents in an academic vascular department are learning complete surgical care, specifically in the context of continuity of care. The retrieved documents from MEDCORP emphasize interprofessional collaboration, care coordination, and team-based approaches in vascular surgery. For example, Document [11] states that after a bypass operation, an interprofessional team (including residents, fellows, nurses, pharmacists, and primary care providers) follows patients through rehabilitation and long-term follow-up. Document [19] highlights that vascular surgeons, anesthesiologists, nurses, and other specialists collaborate throughout the perioperative and postoperative periods. Document [8] and [25] stress that effective communication, structured handoffs, and team-based strategies ensure continuity of care and improve outcomes. These documents consistently describe a team-based, coordinated model of care where residents are part of a multidisciplinary team involved in preoperative planning, intraoperative management, and postoperative follow-up. While the documents do not explicitly state that residents perform complete surgical care independently, they emphasize that trainees are embedded in a system where they learn through team-based, continuous care processes. This implies that residents gain experience in the full spectrum of surgical care through structured, interprofessional, and continuous care models. Therefore, the evidence supports that residents are learning complete surgical care within the context of continuity of care, even if not always performing the full surgical procedure alone. The answer is best supported by 'yes'.", "answer_choice": "A"}
